speaker: interviewer
text: 20, 1995. We're in Studio City, California and the interview's in English and I'm Adelle Chabelski. Tape four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stanley, you were talking about how you were almost hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. So they wanted to hang me, so they took me back, you know, to-- it was a little forest [INAUDIBLE]. And they want to hang me on a tree. And any time they want to hang me, I, I pulled it. I pulled it somehow. I don't-- I don't know. By the way, the, the people who want to hang me, they were Germans, prisoners, too, prisoners, which they were our-- what they call it? [GERMAN], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were German prisoners--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --who were-- who were controlling--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they were inside the camp, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --controlling the camp, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they want to hang me. And I still-- I still-- I still pulled it and I pulled it. Then a German with a-- you know, a German SS came in and said, look at him, he says in German, he's, he's dead already. You know, blood was running down my head and my eyes. He's dead, throw him up on the-- on the-- on the [GERMAN], you know, on the-- on the-- on the hill. So they took me from over there. And they threw me on a-- on a-- on a-- on a big thing of dead people. And that's how I got left. And all the people marched out from the camp. And the same-- late in the afternoon, I remember the sun was so bright, so shiny, I crawled out from over there, from the-- from the camp. And I crawled out and crawling, crawling till I got into a farmer. And I stayed with the farmer for one day and then I crawled to another farm until the American came in. And I thought that I am only the survivor of the-- of, of the-- of the camp. I thought, I'm the only Jewish survivor, because I didn't see anybody. I thought that I am the only survivor. Till the American came in in 19-- in May, I think. May the 1st, the 2nd, I don't remember the date exactly. Till the Americans came, I was, was hiding by the farmers. And I told them that I am a, a Polish, you know, worker and I worked near the camps. I didn't tell them I'm Jewish. Anyway, then, after a week or so, they were talking about going back to Poland where the American set up the camp, American soldiers, and were talking all the-- taking all the people-- workers, Polish workers and Russian workers, who want to be repatriated back to, to homeland. They could go home. So I, I, I was between them and I want to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me on the-- on the truck. And they, they took me to Regensburg. And on the way, I got sick. I got very, very sick. I don't know what it is. They took me to the hospital. And I'd been in the hospital for about four or five weeks in Regensburg under an assumed name. What were your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your symptoms?

speaker: interviewee
text: High fever. Very high fever and dysentery, and high fever. And I came in under an assumed name. I was still afraid to tell them that I am Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH] I told myself that I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what made you pick that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but I'm just looking at the paper now. I remember that I put [POLISH]. And I was under assumed name in the hospital because I thought that I am the only Jew. And after, after being in the hospital, I, I found out there was some Jews from different camps in the hospital, too, which I knew from Plaszow. And from mouth to mouth, I found out that all these people which, which were with me in camp, two days later, they were liberated in Eggenfelden, which is about 30 kilometers from, from, from the Ganacker. They were going by foot, by foot till they-- this is all. The American already were very close. So naturally, I, I found out all my friends are in Eggenfelden. So I took the train-- there was a train at the time-- I went back to Eggenfelden. And I came back. And I-- and they were like, like in a little schoolhouse. Good. All my friends-- few of my friends, they were recuperating. It was like a, a hospital, you know. And they told me they are over there. And I came in. And, and they were walking back. They couldn't believe it that I'm here, because they left me for dead. They couldn't believe that I survived, because everybody marched out from the camp and they left me for dead. And, and as you know, I survived and, and I came and, and we-- since then, we, we were together in that particular town in Eggenfelden.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's quite a reunion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stanley, you know, in the last two hours, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you've told us a lot. And you didn't always show very much emotion. But one of the times when you were very emotional was when you were talking about the, the American planes shooting. Why, why, why that particular moment? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we know that somebody is, is fighting for us. Somebody comes to liberate us. That was-- that was the reason. Somebody's here in the world which, which cares for us. They're going to liberate us. That was the reason. That was the reason. I'm going to be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you really--

speaker: interviewee
text: And any time I, I look today-- I have lots of books which is the history of World War II. Any time I look at the book-- [LAUGHS] funny thing, I'm just looking all these-- all these pages that say about Stalingrad, where they got defeated. Any place the Germans get defeated, that's my pleasure to read [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they get defeated. Any, any place they had success, I don't care too much about it. You know about it. But any place that they-- any chapter that I can see defeated, where they got, you know, thrown out from certain countries, defeated in something, that's my pleasure to read it today. That's the history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that the world didn't care? I mean, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know if they didn't care, the world   didn't care. We knew that we-- we knew that England and, and, and Russia [INAUDIBLE] fighting. But we were left on, on our own. Nobody was fighting for us. Nobody's, nobody's doing anything about it. We didn't know nothing, nothing about what, what, what would happen to us. We didn't have no hope. We had-- we were hoping that the war going to end and we were going to survive, but we didn't see-- we didn't see it. We didn't see it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm still myself surprised how you survived with all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, sometimes I, I, I, I think about it, how we really survived, the small things how I went through, how I survived. As a matter of fact, I want to tell you about in Plaszow. Being in Plaszow, I was lucky to work in the-- in the places, what they call the [GERMAN] that we, we put up barracks. So we were-- we were-- we could-- we could go from place to place, in a way, you know. We could take a hammer or something and said-- if they were-- if we were stopped, we were stopped by the SS or something-- oh, we're going to pick up work here. I'd be sent to do the work here on that barrack. We could-- we could walk around. Not everybody could.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I work-- I work not too far from, from a-- from barracks about-- not too far from-- how they say [POLISH]? How they say? Stables. Stables.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stables. No, not only stables. There were, were cows and, and horses and-- what they call? It is not only stables.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do they call it? They kept-- in Plaszow, they kept pigs and cows, you know, and horses, for the [INAUDIBLE] and stuff like that. And I come and I see in uniform a friend of mine I used to go to school with. He was Polish. And he was in the SS. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was astounded. I didn't know what, what to do, if I can-- I can-- I can ask him something, if he couldn't recognize me? I was afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, I, I had the courage to go to him and I said, [POLISH] do you remember me? I know you. He said, yeah, I remember you. And we, we used to do something that he  brought me some food from, from outside. Not for-- not for, for, for nothing. We gave him some stuff from-- you know, we had in, in, in Plaszow, we had in the camp some stuff which they brought in from the ghettos, you know, some, some clothes, some special, some, you know, bed wear, you know. And they used to take it out. There was a big thing to take it out to, to town. And they could sell it. And they bring some food from out of the-- from, from the city, which he did for me. And that's how, luckily, I, I, I had something that, by coincidence, that I survived. Do you have any health problems after the war? Do you have any now? Did you have any after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I went through a heart attack. And after the, the war, I, well, I, I, I had my, my ulcer, stomach ulcer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arthritis. Well, everybody gets a touch of this. Some would get it from, from the war, some guys are getting something, but I get, get arthritis. And emotionally I am-- you know, you know how it-- how, how it is. It comes back quite often, you know. Anytime we talk about it, especially when we get together with our friends, what do we talk about? We talk about how we went through the camps and how we survived and how it was like before the war, and how the families was together. That's what we talk about. It's a broken, broken, broken life. Like I say, I survived. The only one from my family. The only one. My wife is the only survivor from the whole family. Our kids don't have no aunts, no uncles, no cousins, [SOBS] no cousins, no nothing. So you know what we-- we think about it sometimes. We get together, we reminisce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the life before the war well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. I remember I used to go to-- used to go to school, especially when I was in the last two years of high school, Gymnasium, they used to call it. I used to go and we, we used to go and play, play, in the afternoon play ball, used to go skating. I used to go-- in the winter, I used to go skiing. It was a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you remember your mother, your mother, your father, what do you remember? What scene? How do you see them? What do  you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I see-- most of the time, I see them on a Saturday, Friday, Saturday at the tables, sitting dressed up, you know, going, going, going out on a-- on a walk to the-- to the-- you know, we lived like a [INAUDIBLE] a town which is like a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever I said before? [INAUDIBLE]. I forgot it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Resort.

speaker: interviewee
text: Resort. Like a resort, a little resort. I used to go to the [INAUDIBLE], you know, to walk--

speaker: interviewer
text: A walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the kids, used to go and sit down on the benches. Used to have little, little sandwiches, used to go on Saturday dressed up nice. Used to go, you know, to meet friends, play, play games, play all kinds of different things. It was-- it was a different life. It was a life of family. I had cousins. I had uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I, I was liberated in Germany. I went to Poland, back to Poland. And I, I, I went looking for my family, looking for our friends. And nobody survived. I went to Poland again in 1947. And I-- we used to have a house in Poland. And I sold the house. I sold the house to our neighbors. And then in 1948-- coming back to Germany in 1948, I went to Israel. And I joined the army, the Israeli army in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I I felt great.

speaker: interviewer
text: --state of  Israel was established?

speaker: interviewee
text: It felt great to go to Israel. It felt great to, to be in Israel at that time, you know, that time, you know, in '48, the independence year of Israel. We were young, young people. We were seven-- seven young people going from, from one town to-- from Germany. We were all together, the seven people, in the same group. And it didn't take long, about three months after I, I, I joined the army, after they took me to the army, I got wounded on the front of Lebanon. It was not, not much. But I got wounded. And that was my career in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- I went back to, to, to the city, to Haifa. I was in the hospital for a while. And then I didn't have no trade. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the Israeli government helped out certain people, you know, as a-- as a veteran. And I was a-- somehow, I was a wounded veteran. I couldn't do any particular work, hard work. So they, they-- I, I would like to-- I told them I would like to learn some kind of a trade. So they send me to the Israeli Technion in Haifa, which I learned to be a, a machinist, machinist. And, and, and they call it [? tool ?] maker, anyway. So they teach me, you know, for-- I'd been over there for about seven months. They teach me to run all the-- all the machines, like the lathes and milling machines and shapers and all kind of stuff. They even teach me how to weld-- welding. And after I got out of the school, I couldn't find a job. It was very tough in Israel and I was by myself, didn't have nobody. I had some family which was aunts and uncles, which they emigrated before the war to Israel. So I decided to-- I had an uncle here in, in United States, the one who came from, from England. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I wrote to him, I want to come to the United States. So he, he, he wrote me letters, you know. It's very hard to get out from Israel to United States on the quota, on the quota. I had to go back to Germany and to come back on the-- on the-- on the [INAUDIBLE] quota again. So I went back to Germany in 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, I, I was mated. I met my wife in Germany. And we met in Munich. She was attending the art school in Munich. And in 1951, I came to United States. I didn't do much in Germany. I just waited for papers to come back to be-- to, to get my papers to come to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in the United States. I left because my wife had the papers to come to the United States, so she had to come on her own paper and I had to come on my papers. So we-- I came to the United States in May 1951, and she came in, I think, in August or September, something like that. She will tell you. And we got married in, in November in 1951 in, in the city of Vineland, New Jersey. We had some friends who had chicken farms in Vineland, New Jersey. So we moved to Vineland and we married. And I-- and I came to United States. And I had already somehow-- some kind-- some kind of a trade. I was a machinist. So I got a job in, in some-- in a factory in Vineland, New Jersey, in a glass factory. Used to make some laboratory glass. And luckily, I found some people that they-- that they teach me how to work, because I didn't know that much. And back-- and I worked over there for about a year. And then we moved to Detroit. And that's how-- that's how-- and then from Detroit we came back to-- short story, came back to Vineland, New Jersey, and then came to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those years like right after the war? Right after you came from Israel? Were they difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad. The, the, the, the best years of my life, that time, was in 1945 after I got liberated. Everything was so, so great, so free. We enjoyed the, the the freedom in 1945 and '46. Nothing, nothing mattered, just the freedom, just the freedom to go and to do things and anything. It was great. But coming back from, from Israel was just a waiting period for me to come to the United States. Germany was not the same Germany it was in 1945. Germany was already independent. The, the Germans-- the Germans looked down at the-- at the refugees like, like like, like-- you know? And it was a different Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were just a waiting time. If we wouldn't be coming to United States, I would have gone back to Israel. I couldn't stand to be in Germany. It was tough. Couldn't-- I couldn't be there. So that, that, that was Germany for, for me. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was the happiest guy coming to the United States. That was my happiest day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I, I, I want to be-- I want to be-- to establish myself, to, to do something, to, to, to be something. And I came to United States-- everything I looked at, it was, was great. I loved everything. Especially when I came to New York, the first six months before we move to Vineland, everything was so great. Everything was good. I loved it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I still love it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I knew you were gonna say that. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Life has been good to us. We are very happy. We have a nice-- we have a nice family. We're well-to-do, we're well off, and we are very happy, that's the main thing. And we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will meet your family later, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --could you tell us your sons names and, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Our, our oldest son is Harvey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Detroit. And our second son is Jeffrey. He was born in Detroit. He was born in 1958. And our youngest son is Michael. He was born in, in New Jersey, Vineland, New Jersey. And in 1962, we moved to California. And he was only about six, seven weeks old when we put him in the car and we-- and we moved to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your sons married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the oldest son, Harvey, is not married. Our two sons are married. Jeffrey has two girls and a-- and a boy. The boy is only about two months old now, they have a little boy. They have two girls, twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Twins, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names and what are the wives' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wives' names is Linda-- Jeffery's wife's is [? Linda. ?] And they have two girls, twins, [? Rebecca ?] and [? Danielle. ?] And their little boy, which was born two months ago, his name is [? Steve. ?] He's named after his grandfather from, from his wife's side. His wife's name is Linda [INAUDIBLE]. And Michael is married to [? Sherry ?] and they have two boys. One, one, one boy, his name is [? Kyle. ?] He'll be seven in November. And, and his brother is three years old-- was three years old yesterday, as a matter of fact. His name is [? Shaun. ?] You're going to meet him, I hope, later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your life, your experiences, have affected your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't think so. They affected my children? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nightmares? Dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quite often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I dream most of the time about the family and about me running, running, running, and they, they follow me. They run after me. They, you know-- some crazy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. SS. SS is running after me, running after me. I'm hiding and I'm running. I'm running from the house, the back of our house, you know. This stays in my memory. Like my mother ran away from the house, I guess, and I'm running, you know. It's during-- early in the morning, I'm running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still have those dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I tell you one thing, a funny thing about it. You, you wake up-- when I had my heart attack three years ago, I, I woke up shivering. I, I woke up, I was scared. Somebody's running after me. And, and I woke up and I was sweating, I was scared. And then I had my heart attack. The same night-- in the morning-- I had my heart attack. Same evening. That's what woke me up. And I-- and I-- I woke myself up, I walked to the bathroom, and, you know, I started to breathe harder and harder, but it was, was-- my pain, chest pain was terrible. That's when I had my heart attack. And that's the dream came through, that dream of-- I was so scared, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you were having a heart attack?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, we, we, we knew that we have a heart attack. My wife realized that, she called right away the 911. We realized I had a heart attack, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that we didn't talk about that you would like to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to also to mention to you, I had-- I didn't tell you about Zakopane. I was in this-- the first camp, in the [GERMAN], working camp in Zakopane, the stone quarry. And we were not allowed to go home. Just once a month, we were allowed to go-- certain people were allowed to go home. Not by train. Either by foot or we-- sometimes we rented a driver which worked for the-- for the-- for the German company. And he took us on the-- on the big truck and took us home, and he picked us up the next day. Everybody gave a few zlotys to go home to pick up some clothes and pick up some food which, which were available, because we were getting some food in the, the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, it was in 1941, one day, there was the order given that we were not allowed to go home. And we were quite, quite upset about it and surprised. Why we not go home? We don't-- we don't have no, no, no-- we couldn't-- we washed our own clothes over there, but we had to change clothes somehow. And sometimes we bring something, some little bit of food from the house, and we would like to see the families, too. There was no time to, to write letters or call somebody. You, you didn't-- just didn't do it. So I was elected-- I knew the, the, the meister then. I spoke German. I was elected to go to ask him if he would allow a few people to go home to bring there some clothes or something for, for the rest of us. So I, I went. He lived in a villa. We were not exactly-- there were not barbed wire. You know, we could walk around. And he had a villa outside of the camp. I went to his villa and his name was Metfess, Mr. Metfess, Herr Metfess. It was an Austrian company named [GERMAN]. Anyways, I ask him, I said, Herr Metfess, I'm sorry. I beg you, we would like to go home to pick up some, some, some clothes and some food for the people. Could you let us go? He said, get out of here. Not allowed-- nobody is allowed to leave the camp. So I came back to the barracks. It was already darkish, about 5 o'clock in the-- in the afternoon, the evening. And I told it to the guys, you know. And I, I, I'm going to go to the Bahnhof, to the station, railroad station. I'm going to buy a ticket and go home by, by train. You were not allowed to, to do that. Jews were not allowed to go by train. Period.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but I didn't have no, no star armband, no Jewish star. I'm going to go, nobody's going to recognize me. So I had another two friends, being on the same barrack, and they said let me go with you. Let me just take a shave and I go with you. So we went. And I went to buy the ticket and I bought three tickets. And we went to-- into the train. The train was in the station because it was an end station. City of Zakopane is the end of the-- of the border of the-- of the Polish Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went in the train and we were waiting till the train will start to, to, to, to move to, to our destination, Nowy Targ, which take about maybe 25 minutes by train. So I was sitting, sitting down. And the time went by quite slowly. All of a sudden the door opens and a guy in civilian clothes walks in. He had a little reflector on his-- on his-- on his chest. And he started to scream, where is the Isaak? And he said, where is the-- where is the Isaak, Isaak? We thought that some drunk came in, I thought. And he just walked-- I was sitting. And across from me was sitting a friend of mine, a worker. And he asked him, papers? So he took papers and the name was Avroham Isaak. I remembered the name. So he, he looked at him, he said, you, you are Isaak. [GERMAN]. And he started already to hit him and he said, please. Please, I'm working here in the [GERMAN]. I'm going to go-- we've got to go home. It didn't take long, not even a minute or two. He says, [GERMAN]. Down. He took him down from the train. And he walked down. And, and and we thought-- I thought that no one-- nobody's going to show up again. I thought this is just by coincidence.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, the door opened again. The same guy walks in. And he, instead of coming to me, he made the right turn and he walked to the other compartment. And you could hear through the-- through the compartment, you hear names. And he-- all of a sudden I hear the name, [NON-ENGLISH]. And the same story. [GERMAN]. And the same thing [INAUDIBLE]. And the same thing, he took him-- I guess he took him down, too. I didn't hear anything. And then another few minutes, all of a sudden you hear screams. I am not a Jew. In Polish, I am not a Jew. I am not a Jew. I am not a Jew. And this guy-- all of a sudden, the train starts to move. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It starts-- it starts to move. And, and this guy from the back compartment, with the German, with they've got the guy who screamed I'm not Jew, came running up to my compartment. And he grabbed-- either he grabbed me or he grabbed the seat. He said-- he said Polish, tell the guy I'm not Jew. He talked in Polish. Please tell them I am not Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they stopped with the-- with-- the train with the-- what they call it? The emergency thing. The train stopped right after the station. And they took the guy down. It took about 10 minutes to fix up the, the brakes, you know, the emergency brakes. And I went back home that same night. I was so scared. I was running from the-- from the rail station. I was running home. I came home. And I told my mother what happened. She said, you have to go to the-- to the-- to the Judenrat, to the Jewish council tomorrow morning and tell them the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went. And I told the story, what happened. And I told also the story to the-- to the Jewish community that they should-- anybody who wants to send some packages to the people who work with us, they should bring [? food ?] to the-- to the Jewish community. And I'm going to take them and rent a, a, a, a wagon with a-- with a-- with a horse, you know, with a Polish horse-- to bring anything what it is. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that's the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, she needs to--
